file_name: Ćirković and Stanić - 2024 - Application of the YOLO algorithm for medical purposes in the detection of skin cancer.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the YOLO v8 deep learning algorithm (a neural network) to detect and classify skin moles into benign and malignant categories. The model was trained on a dataset of mole images and evaluated using metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score to assess its performance in dermatological screening.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and validation of a YOLO v8 neural network model on a private dataset of 3000 mole images (1500 benign, 1500 malignant). It reports specific methodology details (training parameters, architecture), results (confusion matrix, precision, recall, F1-score), and conclusions based on this new empirical data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Application of the YOLO algorithm for Medical Purposes in the Detection of Skin Cancer
journal: 10th International Scientific Conference Session: Computer Sciences and Information Technology
brief_summary: This study applied the YOLO v8 deep learning algorithm to detect and classify skin moles as benign or malignant using a private dataset of 3000 annotated mole images. The model was trained on 2400 images and evaluated on a validation set of 248 images to assess its potential for preliminary dermatological screening. Performance metrics included precision, recall, and F1-score, with the model achieving an overall precision of 78.2% and a recall of 81.6% on the validation set. Specifically, the precision was 84.5% for benign lesions and 71.8% for malignant lesions. The authors conclude that while the results are acceptable for preliminary screening, the system requires dermatologist supervision and further improvements to enhance precision.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the private dataset of 3000 mole images (1500 benign, 1500 malignant). The model achieved a precision of 84.5% and recall of 82.6% for this class on the validation set.

One of the two classes in the private dataset of 3000 mole images (1500 benign, 1500 malignant). The model achieved a precision of 71.8% and recall of 80.7% for this class on the validation set. Contextually linked to skin cancer/melanoma in the introduction.

all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign or malignant) for skin moles. The study uses a private dataset of 3000 images (1500 benign, 1500 malignant) and evaluates performance using precision, recall, and F1-score for these binary classes. Although YOLO is an object detection algorithm that outputs bounding boxes, the specific application here is classifying the detected lesions into benign or malignant classes for screening purposes.

datasets_used.name: Private Mole Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of annotated mole images collected and annotated in accordance with ethical standards. Images were annotated using the Labelme tool and converted to TXT format for YOLO training. Images not suitable for annotation were excluded.

use.training: Used to train the YOLO v8 model (2400 images, 80% of total).
use.validation: Used to evaluate model performance (248 images, 10% of total).
use.testing: Used for testing (352 images, 10% of total).

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices.

Private Mole Dataset (Validation Set).description: Evaluation on a validation set of 248 images (132 benign, 120 malignant) using YOLO v8.
Private Mole Dataset (Validation Set).notes: The model achieved 100% precision at a high confidence threshold (0.848), meaning no false positives were recorded at that specific threshold. At a low confidence threshold (0.000), recall was 99%. The results are considered acceptable for preliminary screening but require dermatologist supervision.

Private Mole Dataset (Confusion Matrix A.description: Detailed classification counts from the confusion matrix analysis presented in the paper.
Private Mole Dataset (Confusion Matrix A.notes: The model did not identify any background instances as true positives. The text notes that failing to identify background instances is acceptable in this context.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the YOLO v8 model used in this study; however, the paper notes that YOLO backbones are typically trained on large datasets such as ImageNet.

architecture_details.backbone_description: Convolutional neural network responsible for feature extraction; commonly used backbones include VGG16, ResNet50, CSPDarknet53, and EfficientNet.
architecture_details.neck_description: Connects backbone and head using feature pyramid aggregation techniques such as PANet, Spatial Pyramid Pooling (SPP), Feature Pyramid Network (FPN), NAS-FPN, and Rep-PAN.
architecture_details.head_description: Consists of predictive layers generating final classifications; each level (P2 to P5) is connected to specific detection blocks that predict bounding boxes and object classes.

preprocessing_techniques: Annotation using Labelme tool to create JSON files with coordinate points
Conversion of annotations from JSON to TXT format using labelme2yolo tool
Exclusion of images not suitable for annotation
conclusions: The YOLO v8 algorithm achieved acceptable performance for preliminary dermatological screening, with an overall precision of 78.2% and recall of 81.6% on the validation set.
The model demonstrated higher precision for benign lesions (84.5%) compared to malignant lesions (71.8%).
At a high confidence threshold of 0.848, the model achieved 100% precision, indicating no false positives at that specific threshold.
The system is suitable for preliminary screening but requires supervision by dermatologists for definitive diagnosis.
The application of YOLO v8 has the potential to facilitate early intervention, save time, and reduce healthcare costs by enabling accessible self-monitoring tools.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, private dataset of only 3,000 images (1,500 benign, 1,500 malignant) with no external validation, limiting generalizability.
The validation set is very small (248 images), which may lead to unstable performance estimates.
The model was trained on a single hardware device (NVIDIA GeForce 1080) for a short duration (4 hours 10 minutes), raising concerns about overfitting or insufficient training.
The paper acknowledges that the overall precision (78.2%) is 'not impressive' and that the system requires dermatologist supervision for definitive diagnosis.
There is a notable discrepancy in the reported validation set size: the text states 248 images, but Table 2 lists 252 instances (132 benign + 120 malignant).

The dataset includes a 'Background' class, but the model failed to identify any true positive background instances, which the authors deem acceptable but represents a limitation in object detection capability.
No statistical significance testing (p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are based solely on descriptive metrics.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, or learning rate schedule.
Data augmentation techniques are not reported, which is a significant omission for deep learning studies on medical imaging.
The study is published in a conference proceedings rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may affect the rigor of the review process.
